concept: Nestle / Snuggle / Settling Close and Soft
description: To nestle: to settle the body close against warmth and stay there. The child against the shoulder, the cat into the blanket's fold, the toy tucked under a sleeping chin. 'Numea' is the body's way of saying it is home: small adjustments toward closeness until nothing more adjusts, trust performed in the muscles.
sound_symbolism: 'Nu' is the night-soft opening of 'nulae' (sleep) and 'nuwera' (bed), the household's darkest, warmest sounds. 'Me' makes the small settling adjustment, and final 'a' opens into rest. The first two syllables remain shared with 'numelo' (nurture) on purpose: nestling is nurture answered from inside the arms.
grammatical_notes: Intransitive verb, its place or companion given by prepositions: 'misheko pai thero numea.' (The cat nestles near the fire.) 'tupiwa phou lopia menoa numea.' (The rabbit nestles under the child's face.) The event noun comes by rule. Kin to 'numelo' (nurture) and 'lomare' (embrace), the shelter it accepts.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Safety has a posture, and this is its verb: closeness sought and granted without a word exchanged.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: One hearth, shared warmth, bodies arranged around it on a winter night: the oldest architecture of comfort.
